BigQuery export and Looker Studio is a heavyweight alternative for a use case natively supported by Cloud Monitoring dashboards. Cloud Monitoring does support multi-SLO dashboards.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Your organization needs to verify that Cloud Logging is receiving all expected audit log types from a specific GCP project. Which approach gives you a definitive inventory of which audit log types are enabled for the project?
options: Check the Cloud Logging log explorer for the presence of any audit log entries in the last 7 days
Run gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID and inspect the auditConfigs section, which shows which log types (ADMIN_READ, DATA_READ, DATA_WRITE) are enabled per service
Query Cloud Asset Inventory for the project's IAM policy resource and look for audit log configuration attributes
Open the Cloud Console IAM page and check the 'Audit Logs' tab for each service to see enabled log types
questions.explanation: Audit log type configuration is stored in the project's IAM policy in the auditConfigs section. Running gcloud projects get-iam-policy returns the full IAM policy including auditConfigs, which shows exactly which services have which audit log types (ADMIN_READ, DATA_READ, DATA_WRITE) enabled or disabled. Admin Activity logs are always enabled and do not appear in auditConfigs. Checking the log explorer only shows what has been logged recently, not what is configured to be logged. Cloud Asset Inventory can retrieve IAM policy state but gcloud is more direct. The Cloud Console IAM Audit Logs tab shows the same information visually but is not as scriptable for verification.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Your SLO has a 30-day compliance window and a 99.5% target. The error budget is 0.5% of 30 days = 216 minutes. It is the 20th day of the month and your monitoring shows 180 minutes of downtime has occurred. What percentage of the error budget has been consumed, and should you be concerned?
options: 180/216 = 83.3% of the budget consumed in 66.7% of the window; the consumption rate is faster than the budget allows, which is a warning signal requiring attention
180/216 = 83.3% of the budget consumed; this is acceptable because more than 80% of the month has passed
180 minutes consumed out of 216 minutes; the budget will automatically reset at day 21 so no action is needed
The SLO has not been breached because 36 minutes of budget remains; no action is needed until the budget is fully exhausted
questions.explanation: At day 20 of 30 (66.7% of the window), a proportionally even burn rate would have consumed 66.7% of the budget (144 minutes). Instead, 83.3% (180 minutes) has been consumed, meaning the service is burning budget faster than the linear rate. With only 36 minutes of budget remaining and 10 days left in the window, any further significant downtime will breach the SLO. This warrants attention: the team should assess what caused the 180 minutes of downtime, whether the rate is continuing, and whether a deployment freeze or reliability sprint is needed. Error budgets do not reset mid-window; the 30-day rolling window means the budget is measured continuously.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: A developer asks how to view the logs from a specific GKE pod named checkout-7f9d8b-xkz45 in the us-central1-a zone using Cloud Logging. Which log filter syntax retrieves logs from that specific pod?
options: resource.type="k8s_container" AND resource.labels.pod_name="checkout-7f9d8b-xkz45"
jsonPayload.pod_name="checkout-7f9d8b-xkz45" AND resource.type="gke_cluster"
labels.k8s-pod/app="checkout" AND zone="us-central1-a"
log_name="projects/PROJECT_ID/logs/pods/checkout-7f9d8b-xkz45"
questions.explanation: GKE container logs use the monitored resource type k8s_container. The resource labels include pod_name, namespace_name, cluster_name, container_name, and location. To filter logs from a specific pod, use resource.type="k8s_container" AND resource.labels.pod_name="checkout-7f9d8b-xkz45". The resource labels are indexed and efficient to filter on. jsonPayload.pod_name is an application-defined field, not the resource label. The gke_cluster resource type represents cluster-level logs, not container logs. The log_name structure for GKE pods does not include the pod name in the log name path.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Your team wants to measure the business impact of reliability incidents. Specifically, you want to track how many checkout transactions were lost during downtime periods by correlating transaction volume metrics with incident windows. Which combination of GCP tools enables this?
options: Cloud Monitoring incident history and Cloud Monitoring custom metrics for transaction count; use the Cloud Monitoring dashboard to overlay the two time series and manually estimate the drop

Cloud Monitoring alerting policy incident history (to identify start/end times of downtime) and Cloud Monitoring or BigQuery metrics for transaction volume; compute the delta between expected transaction rate (baseline from pre-incident window) and actual transactions during the incident to estimate lost transactions
Cloud Logging error count metrics; divide error count by the typical transaction success rate to estimate lost transactions
Cloud Deploy rollout history, which shows the exact number of transactions affected by each deployment-related incident
questions.explanation: To quantify lost transactions, you need two data points: (1) when downtime occurred (from Cloud Monitoring incident start/end times or SLO burn period) and (2) the transaction volume shortfall during that window. By comparing the actual transaction count metric during the incident to the expected baseline (e.g., same time window the previous week from Cloud Monitoring or BigQuery), you can estimate lost transactions. This method is used in SRE practice to convert reliability metrics into business impact. Overlaying time series manually is imprecise. Error count divided by success rate is indirect and less accurate. Cloud Deploy tracks deployment events, not transaction volumes.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Your team is implementing observability for a new Cloud Bigtable-backed service. Reads are slow and you suspect hotspotting on specific row key ranges. Which GCP observability tool helps identify hotspot row key ranges in Bigtable?
options: Cloud Profiler -- attach it to the application; it will show which row keys cause the most CPU usage
Cloud Bigtable Key Visualizer -- it provides a heat map visualization of read and write throughput by row key range over time, making hotspots visually obvious
Cloud Trace -- it records the row key in each Bigtable read span attribute, which you can aggregate to find frequent keys
Cloud Monitoring bigtable/server/latency metric filtered by row_key label to identify slow row keys
questions.explanation: Cloud Bigtable Key Visualizer is a built-in tool in the Cloud Console that renders a heat map of read and write activity across all row key ranges over a selected time window. Hotspots appear as bright bands in the heat map, immediately showing which row key ranges are receiving disproportionate traffic. This is the authoritative tool for diagnosing Bigtable row key design problems. Cloud Profiler shows application-level CPU usage but does not have visibility into Bigtable row key patterns. Cloud Trace can record span attributes but requires custom instrumentation per key and does not aggregate across key ranges. Cloud Monitoring Bigtable metrics do not include a row_key label on the latency metric.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Your GKE deployment experiences traffic spikes every weekday at 9 AM when users log in. Pod count needs to scale from 5 to 40 within 3 minutes. Currently, scaling is slow because new nodes must be provisioned. Which combination of features minimizes scale-out latency?
options: Enable Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) with CPU-based scaling and rely on the cluster autoscaler to provision new nodes on demand

Enable HPA for pod scaling, enable the cluster autoscaler with node auto-provisioning, and configure node pool overprovisioning by deploying low-priority placeholder pods that are evicted when real pods need capacity
Increase the minimum node count of the node pool to 40 so capacity is always available
Use Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) to increase CPU and memory limits on existing pods instead of scaling out
questions.explanation: Fast scale-out requires both pod and node capacity to be available quickly. HPA handles pod scheduling decisions. Cluster autoscaler adds nodes when pods are unschedulable, but node provisioning takes 2-4 minutes. The overprovisioning pattern deploys low-priority placeholder pods that consume node capacity; when real pods need to schedule, the placeholders are evicted immediately, freeing existing node capacity without waiting for new nodes. This allows pods to schedule within seconds. Keeping 40 minimum nodes is wasteful and expensive overnight. VPA adjusts resource limits vertically per pod but does not increase pod count to handle more concurrent load.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your Cloud Run service processes image uploads. Each invocation downloads a large ML model (500MB) from Cloud Storage at startup, taking 45 seconds. This causes severe cold start latency. Which TWO strategies MOST effectively reduce cold start time for this workload? (Choose TWO)
options: Set the minimum instances to a non-zero value so at least N instances are always warm and the model is already loaded
Store the model in a Cloud Storage bucket mounted via Cloud Storage FUSE inside the Cloud Run container
Bundle the ML model directly into the container image so it is available at container start without a network download
Increase the Cloud Run request timeout to 300 seconds to accommodate the 45-second startup
questions.explanation: Two complementary strategies work here: (1) setting minimum instances keeps warm instances alive between requests, completely eliminating cold starts for most traffic; (2) bundling the model in the container image means even cold-started instances have the model on local disk and avoid the 45-second network download. Cloud Storage FUSE mounts are lazy-loaded and add I/O overhead; they do not eliminate the download latency and may make it worse. Increasing the timeout accommodates the slow startup but does not fix the underlying latency problem for users waiting on cold starts.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud Run service processes requests that each spawn 50 lightweight goroutines. The service is CPU-bound. Current configuration is 1 vCPU and concurrency of 80. You observe that CPU is pegged at 100% and requests are queueing. Which configuration change provides the most direct performance improvement?
options: Increase the concurrency setting from 80 to 1000 to allow more simultaneous requests
Increase the CPU allocation to 4 vCPUs and adjust concurrency to match the workload's parallelism capacity; more CPU allows the service to process more concurrent requests efficiently
Reduce the concurrency to 1 so each instance handles only one request at a time, reducing CPU contention
Switch to Cloud Run jobs which have higher CPU limits than Cloud Run services
questions.explanation: The service is CPU-bound: 100% CPU utilization means the single vCPU cannot process requests fast enough. Increasing to 4 vCPUs gives the service more processing capacity for its goroutines. Concurrency should be tuned accordingly -- if each request uses significant CPU, allowing fewer concurrent requests per instance (so each gets more CPU time) may also help, but the primary fix is adding CPU capacity. Increasing concurrency beyond CPU capacity deepens the queue without providing more throughput. Reducing concurrency to 1 limits throughput further. Cloud Run jobs are for batch workloads, not serving HTTP requests.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your application on GKE is experiencing variable load. During peak hours, pods use 800m CPU but the Deployment requests 200m CPU per pod. The HPA is set to target 80% CPU utilization. Pods are being evicted by the cluster due to resource contention during peaks. What is the BEST fix?
options: Increase the CPU request to 1000m per pod to match or exceed the actual peak usage; this ensures pods are scheduled on nodes with sufficient capacity and are not evicted under load
Remove the CPU request entirely so pods can use any available CPU without reservation
Reduce the HPA target utilization to 50% so more pods are created before CPU is fully saturated
Switch to Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) in auto mode; it will increase CPU limits automatically during peaks
questions.explanation: Kubernetes scheduling and eviction decisions are based on pod resource requests, not limits. Pods with low requests are placed on nodes that may not have sufficient actual CPU, leading to throttling and contention. When the node is under pressure, pods with low requests are the first candidates for eviction. Setting the CPU request to match or slightly exceed the expected peak usage (1000m or higher) ensures the scheduler places pods on nodes with adequate capacity. Removing requests makes scheduling unpredictable. Lowering HPA target creates more pods but each pod is still undersized. VPA in auto mode can help but it restarts pods to apply new resource values, which may not be acceptable during peaks.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your GKE-based event-driven worker service consumes messages from a Pub/Sub subscription. During peak hours, the subscription backlog grows large and you need to scale out workers quickly based on queue depth, not CPU. Which autoscaling approach should you use?
options: HPA with a CPU target of 20% so workers scale out aggressively before CPU is saturated
KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaler) with a Pub/Sub scaler configured to scale based on the number of unacked messages in the subscription
Cluster autoscaler with node auto-provisioning; it detects pending pods and scales the cluster
VPA with a memory-based policy; Pub/Sub consumer memory grows with backlog size
questions.explanation: KEDA is specifically designed for event-driven scaling. Its Pub/Sub scaler reads the subscription's unacked message count (oldest_unacked_message_age or num_undelivered_messages) and scales the Deployment replicas proportionally to the queue depth. This provides direct, queue-depth-driven autoscaling without proxying through CPU or memory metrics. HPA with low CPU target is an indirect proxy that may not correlate well with queue depth. The cluster autoscaler handles node-level scaling but needs pod-level scaling (like KEDA) to create the pods that trigger node provisioning. VPA adjusts resource limits, not replica count.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your application serves a globally distributed user base with static assets (JS, CSS, images) that change only on deployments (every few weeks). Users in Asia report slow page loads because assets are served from a single us-central1 origin. Which GCP service eliminates most of the latency for these users?
options: Deploy additional Cloud Run instances in Asia-Pacific regions and update DNS to use geolocation routing
Enable Cloud CDN on the Cloud Load Balancing backend serving the static assets; Cloud CDN caches content at Google's global edge PoPs, serving assets from the nearest edge location
Compress the static assets with Brotli encoding to reduce transfer size
Increase the origin server's egress bandwidth limit to support faster cross-Pacific transfers
questions.explanation: Cloud CDN integrates with Cloud Load Balancing to cache cacheable responses at Google's global edge Points of Presence (PoPs). Users in Asia receive assets from the nearest edge PoP rather than from the us-central1 origin, eliminating most of the transcontinental round-trip latency. For static assets that rarely change, CDN is the highest-impact optimization. Deploying additional instances handles compute load but is more expensive and does not cache static assets as efficiently. Compression reduces transfer size but not round-trip latency. Increasing origin bandwidth helps throughput but not latency for distant users.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: You deployed a new version of your service and Cloud CDN is still serving stale cached content to users. You need to immediately invalidate the cache for all paths under /api/v2/. Which Cloud CDN action achieves this?
options: Redeploy the Cloud Run service; redeployment automatically invalidates all associated CDN cache entries
Use the gcloud compute url-maps invalidate-cdn-cache command (or the Cloud Console Cache Invalidation feature) with the path pattern /api/v2/* to purge all cached entries matching that prefix
Wait for the CDN cache TTL to expire; Cloud CDN does not support manual invalidation
Delete and recreate the load balancer backend service to flush all cached content
questions.explanation: Cloud CDN supports cache invalidation via the gcloud compute url-maps invalidate-cdn-cache command or the Cloud Console. You specify a path prefix or pattern (e.g., /api/v2/*) and Cloud CDN purges all cached content matching that path from all edge PoPs. This takes effect within a few minutes. Redeployment does not trigger automatic CDN invalidation. Cloud CDN does support manual invalidation -- it is a key operational feature. Deleting and recreating the backend service is an extreme and disruptive approach that would cause downtime.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your e-commerce application reads product catalog data on every request. The catalog is stored in Cloud Spanner and contains 500,000 products. Catalog data changes only when products are added or removed (a few times per day). Read latency from Spanner is 20ms per query. You want to reduce latency to under 2ms for catalog reads. Which caching solution is MOST appropriate?
options: Increase Cloud Spanner node count to reduce read latency through more distributed processing
Enable Cloud Spanner stale reads with a 15-second staleness; this reduces latency by reading from the nearest replica

Introduce a Cloud Memorystore for Redis cache in front of Spanner; cache full catalog query results with a TTL aligned to the update frequency; serve reads from Redis and fall through to Spanner on cache miss
Use Cloud CDN to cache Spanner query responses at the edge
questions.explanation: For data that changes infrequently (a few times per day), an in-memory cache like Cloud Memorystore for Redis delivers sub-millisecond read latency by eliminating the database query for the majority of requests. Setting a TTL of minutes to hours and invalidating the cache when catalog changes occur gives excellent performance while ensuring freshness. Cloud Spanner node scaling reduces latency but cannot achieve sub-2ms for 500K-row queries. Stale reads reduce latency somewhat (to ~5ms) but not to sub-2ms. Cloud CDN caches HTTP responses, not raw database query results; applying it to Spanner queries requires additional infrastructure.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your GKE application connects to a Cloud SQL PostgreSQL database. Under high load, you observe that the application opens thousands of short-lived connections and the database CPU spikes to 90% even though query throughput is modest. What is the MOST likely cause and fix?
options: The database needs more vCPUs; scale up the Cloud SQL instance to a larger machine type

The application is not using connection pooling; each request opens and closes a new database connection, overwhelming the database with connection overhead; deploy PgBouncer (Cloud SQL Auth Proxy with pooling or a sidecar) to pool connections
The Cloud SQL instance is in a different region than the GKE cluster, causing high latency per query
The application needs read replicas; all reads should be redirected to replicas to reduce load on the primary
questions.explanation: PostgreSQL connection establishment is expensive: each new connection requires authentication, process forking, and memory allocation. Thousands of short-lived connections generate massive CPU overhead disproportionate to the actual query load. Connection pooling (via PgBouncer, the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy's built-in pooling mode, or an application-level pool) multiplexes many application connections onto a small set of persistent database connections, dramatically reducing the connection overhead. Scaling up the instance treats the symptom. Cross-region latency would appear as high query latency, not CPU spikes. Read replicas distribute read load but do not solve connection overhead on the primary.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your application queries Cloud Firestore and the team notices that some collection queries are extremely slow (10+ seconds) when filtering by a field that is not the document ID. Cloud Logging shows a warning: 'This query requires an index'. What is the correct fix?
options: Switch to Cloud Datastore mode; Firestore in Datastore mode does not require indexes
Create a composite index or single-field index in Firestore for the queried field and collection combination; Firestore requires explicit indexes for queries that filter or sort on non-ID fields
Increase Firestore read capacity units to handle the high-latency queries faster
Restructure the data to use a flat collection per filter value, eliminating the need for the index
questions.explanation: Cloud Firestore in Native mode requires indexes for queries that filter or order on fields other than the document ID. Without an index, Firestore must do a full collection scan, which is both slow and returns a warning in logs. Creating the appropriate index (single-field or composite, depending on the query) allows Firestore to execute the query efficiently using the index B-tree. Cloud Datastore mode also requires indexes for non-ancestor queries. There are no Firestore 'capacity units' that can be scaled. Restructuring data to avoid indexes is a design workaround but is not always practical.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your Cloud Spanner database is experiencing hotspot issues: one node handles disproportionate write load. Investigation shows that all row keys are prefixed with a sequential timestamp. What schema change resolves this?
options: Increase the number of Cloud Spanner nodes to distribute the hotspot load across more nodes
Hash or reverse the timestamp in the row key prefix, or use UUID-based keys, so that writes are distributed uniformly across all Spanner splits instead of sequentially hitting the same split
Add more columns to the table to increase row size, which forces Cloud Spanner to split rows more frequently
Use Cloud Bigtable instead of Cloud Spanner; Bigtable handles sequential timestamp keys better
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner distributes data across splits by key range. Sequential timestamp-prefixed keys cause all new writes to hit the same end-of-range split (hotspot), because Spanner always writes to the current maximum key. Reversing the timestamp bits, hashing the key prefix, or using random UUIDs distributes writes uniformly across all splits. Adding more nodes increases overall capacity but does not eliminate the hotspot -- the bottleneck is the single split receiving all writes. Cloud Bigtable also suffers from sequential key hotspots; this is a general distributed database problem, not Spanner-specific.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your GKE pods running a Node.js API service are consuming significantly more memory over time, eventually causing OOM kills after 6-8 hours. Cloud Profiler is enabled. Which Cloud Profiler profile type should you collect to diagnose the memory leak?
options: CPU time profile -- identifies functions that consume the most processor time
Heap profile -- shows which objects are allocated in heap memory and which functions retain the most memory, identifying the source of memory growth
Wall time profile -- shows elapsed time per function including I/O wait, which may indicate slow memory deallocation
Thread contention profile -- shows which threads are waiting for locks, indicating potential memory-related deadlocks
questions.explanation: A heap profile captures the in-use heap allocations at sampling time, showing which data structures and which code paths are responsible for retaining memory. Comparing heap profiles taken at different times reveals which allocations are growing, pointing directly to the memory leak. CPU time profiles show processor usage, not memory retention. Wall time includes I/O wait and is useful for latency analysis, not memory leak detection. Thread contention profiles diagnose synchronization issues, not memory growth.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your Cloud Run service handles both short-lived API requests (under 100ms) and long-running report generation requests (up to 5 minutes). The long-running requests are causing timeouts for API requests because they share the same concurrency pool. What is the BEST architectural change?
options: Increase the Cloud Run request timeout to 5 minutes for the combined service

Separate the two workloads into two Cloud Run services: one optimized for low-latency API requests (low concurrency, fast timeout) and one for report generation (higher timeout, lower concurrency, possibly using Cloud Run jobs for true batch work)
Set Cloud Run concurrency to 1 so API and report requests never share a container instance
Increase the number of minimum instances so more containers are available to handle both workload types simultaneously
questions.explanation: Mixing latency-sensitive and long-running workloads in the same service creates interference: long-running requests hold concurrency slots, increasing latency for API requests even when they are otherwise fast. Separating them into two Cloud Run services allows independent scaling, timeout configuration, and concurrency tuning for each workload profile. Report generation is also a natural fit for Cloud Run jobs (asynchronous, not HTTP-triggered). Setting concurrency to 1 makes the problem worse by reducing throughput for both types. Adding minimum instances helps availability but not the contention between workload types.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your team is optimizing a GKE workload for cost and performance. The current pods use 2 CPU and 4GB memory requests, but Cloud Monitoring shows they actually use 0.3 CPU and 0.8GB on average. Which Kubernetes feature automatically right-sizes pod resource requests based on observed usage?
options: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) -- it reduces pod count when utilization is low, effectively right-sizing
Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) -- it analyzes historical resource usage and recommends or automatically updates CPU and memory requests to match actual consumption
Cluster autoscaler -- it resizes nodes based on workload demand, indirectly right-sizing pods
LimitRange -- it sets maximum resource limits per namespace, preventing over-allocation
questions.explanation: Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) monitors the historical CPU and memory usage of pods and provides recommendations (in recommendation mode) or automatically updates the resource requests in the pod spec (in auto mode) to match actual consumption. This prevents over-provisioning (wasted capacity and cost) and under-provisioning (throttling and OOM). HPA adjusts replica count, not per-pod resource sizing. The cluster autoscaler works at the node level, not the pod resource level. LimitRange sets caps but does not right-size based on usage history.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: You are building a session management system for a high-traffic web application deployed on GKE. Session data is small (under 1KB per session) but must be read on every request with sub-millisecond latency. Cloud Memorystore for Redis is the chosen caching layer. Which Redis data structure and access pattern is MOST appropriate for storing and retrieving session data?
options: Store each session as a Redis String with the session ID as the key and a JSON-serialized object as the value; use GET and SET with an expiry (SETEX) to read and write sessions with automatic TTL-based expiration
Store all sessions in a single Redis Hash where the field is the session ID; retrieve all sessions with HGETALL on every request
Store sessions in a Redis List (one list per user); append new session data with RPUSH and read with LRANGE
Use Redis Pub/Sub to broadcast session updates to all application pods simultaneously
questions.explanation: For session storage, a String per session (key = session_id, value = serialized session data) with SETEX (set with expiry) is the standard, optimal Redis pattern. GET and SETEX are O(1) operations with predictable sub-millisecond latency. The TTL handles session expiration automatically without background cleanup jobs. Storing all sessions in one Hash with HGETALL retrieves all sessions on every request, which is O(N) and catastrophically slow for many sessions. Redis Lists are ordered sequences, not key-value lookups, and are inappropriate for session access by ID. Pub/Sub is a messaging pattern, not a storage pattern.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your GKE Deployment has a readinessProbe configured with initialDelaySeconds: 5. A newly deployed pod's application takes 45 seconds to initialize before it can handle requests. During the 40-second gap (after the first readiness check fails), the pod receives traffic and returns errors. What configuration change resolves this?
options: Remove the readinessProbe entirely so the pod is considered ready immediately upon starting

Increase initialDelaySeconds to 50 seconds, or use a startupProbe with a sufficient failureThreshold*periodSeconds to cover the initialization period; the pod will not receive traffic until the readiness probe passes
Add a livenessProbe with the same initialDelaySeconds to restart the pod faster if it is not ready
Set terminationGracePeriodSeconds to 50 so the pod has more time to become ready before being terminated
questions.explanation: A readinessProbe controls whether a pod receives traffic from a Service. With initialDelaySeconds: 5, the first readiness check runs 5 seconds after the container starts, which is 40 seconds before the application is ready. If the probe fails, the pod is removed from the Service endpoint list; however, if the probe runs too early and the application is not yet listening, Kubernetes may briefly add the pod to endpoints between probe cycles. The correct fix is to set initialDelaySeconds to at least 50 seconds, or better, use a startupProbe (designed specifically for slow-starting containers) with a failureThreshold that covers the startup window. Removing the readiness probe would route traffic to unready pods. A livenessProbe controls restart behavior, not traffic routing. terminationGracePeriodSeconds controls shutdown, not startup.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your application makes 50 independent reads to Cloud Firestore on each request, executing them serially. The service latency is dominated by these sequential reads (each taking ~5ms = 250ms total). How do you reduce this latency without changing the data model?
options: Use Firestore transactions to batch all 50 reads into a single atomic operation

Execute all 50 Firestore reads in parallel using concurrent goroutines, async/await, or Promise.all() in the application code; total latency becomes approximately 1 read's latency (~5ms) rather than 50x5ms = 250ms
Enable Firestore offline persistence so the client caches all reads locally
Move the 50 documents into a single Firestore document as an array field to reduce the read count to 1
questions.explanation: The reads are independent, so there is no correctness reason to execute them sequentially. Parallelizing all 50 reads using the language's concurrency primitives (goroutines, async/await, Promise.all) means they execute simultaneously against Firestore; total latency drops from 50 * 5ms = 250ms to approximately one round-trip (~5ms). Firestore transactions are for atomic multi-document writes or reads that must be consistent relative to each other; they do not batch reads into a single network call. Offline persistence helps offline scenarios but adds stale data risk in a server application. Merging documents into an array changes the data model and breaks the ability to read individual documents independently.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your GKE cluster is under-utilizing nodes: average CPU utilization is 15% across all nodes. You want to reduce node count and cost while maintaining workload availability. Which GCP feature should you enable?
options: Manually reduce the node pool size by deleting nodes; Kubernetes will reschedule pods automatically

Enable the cluster autoscaler with a minimum node count of 1; it will automatically remove underutilized nodes and reschedule their pods to the remaining nodes, subject to PodDisruptionBudget constraints
Enable node auto-provisioning to create a new smaller node pool; delete the old pool manually
Switch all workloads to spot VMs to reduce cost without changing node count
questions.explanation: The cluster autoscaler monitors node utilization and removes nodes that are underutilized (all their pods can be rescheduled onto other nodes) subject to PodDisruptionBudgets and other constraints. Enabling it with a minimum count ensures baseline capacity is maintained. Scale-down is automatic and safe for properly configured workloads. Manual node deletion is risky and does not respect PodDisruptionBudgets. Node auto-provisioning creates and removes entire node pools dynamically but requires defining resource limits; it is more complex than enabling autoscaler on an existing pool. Spot VMs reduce the per-node cost but do not reduce node count.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your team uses Cloud Load Balancing with a global external Application Load Balancer. The backend GKE service returns large JSON responses (2-10MB) and backend CPU is high from serialization. Cloud CDN is not applicable because responses are user-specific. Which load balancer feature can reduce backend CPU and latency for these large responses?
options: Enable Cloud CDN on the backend service; personalized responses can be cached with a Vary header
Enable gzip or Brotli response compression in the backend application; the load balancer transparently forwards compressed responses to clients that advertise Accept-Encoding support
Increase the load balancer's connection timeout to allow more time for large response serialization
Switch to a regional internal Application Load Balancer which has lower latency for large responses
questions.explanation: Enabling compression (gzip or Brotli) in the application compresses large JSON responses before transmission. A 2-10MB JSON payload typically compresses to 10-30% of its original size, significantly reducing bandwidth and transfer time. Backend CPU for compression is lower than for transferring raw bytes over the wire, and the client decompresses efficiently. Cloud CDN with Vary headers does not work reliably for truly personalized responses (different per user session). Connection timeouts do not affect serialization CPU. Switching to a regional internal load balancer changes routing topology but does not reduce response size.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your team is designing a Cloud Spanner-backed application that will execute large analytical queries alongside transactional workloads. You are concerned about analytical queries consuming all read capacity and impacting transaction latency. Which Cloud Spanner feature addresses this?
options: Use Cloud Spanner partitioned queries for analytics; they run on separate infrastructure from transactional reads
Create a Cloud Spanner backup before running analytical queries to protect the data
Configure request priority on analytical queries using RequestOptions.priority = LOW; transactional queries set MEDIUM or HIGH priority so they get preference under contention
Route analytical queries to a Cloud Spanner read replica instance in a different region
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner supports request priority (HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW) via RequestOptions at the query or transaction level. Setting analytical queries to LOW priority ensures they yield CPU and I/O resources to MEDIUM or HIGH priority transactional queries under contention. This allows both workload types to coexist on the same instance without analytical queries starving transactions. Partitioned queries improve query parallelism but do not isolate resources. Creating a backup is unrelated to query performance isolation. Cloud Spanner does not have a traditional read replica model in the way relational databases do; its multi-region configurations replicate for availability and regional read performance.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: You are profiling a Go service on GKE using Cloud Profiler and notice that a function called parseUserData is consuming 60% of CPU time. This function deserializes the same user record on every request, but the user data changes at most once per hour. What optimization would have the GREATEST impact?
options: Rewrite parseUserData in C and use CGO bindings to call it from Go
Cache the deserialized user record in Cloud Memorystore for Redis or in an in-process cache (e.g., a sync.Map with TTL) so parseUserData is called only once per hour per user, not on every request
Increase the Cloud Run CPU allocation to reduce the impact of the expensive function
Run parseUserData in a separate goroutine so it does not block the request handler
questions.explanation: The function consumes 60% of CPU deserializing data that changes infrequently. Caching the result -- either in-process (in a time-aware map) or in Redis (shared across pods) -- means the expensive deserialization runs at most once per hour per user, and all subsequent requests serve from cache at negligible cost. This directly eliminates the bottleneck identified by Cloud Profiler. Rewriting in C is a major engineering effort for a problem solvable by caching. Increasing CPU allocation is expensive and treats the symptom. Running in a background goroutine makes it non-blocking but the CPU usage still accumulates.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your Cloud Run service is configured with concurrency 80 and you are observing 429 Too Many Requests errors under heavy load. Cloud Monitoring shows maximum_concurrent_requests is regularly hitting 80 per instance. What are TWO valid approaches to resolve this?
options: Increase the Cloud Run service concurrency setting above 80 if the application can handle more concurrent requests without degrading; this allows each instance to serve more requests before a new instance is spun up
Decrease concurrency to 1 to prevent request queuing within an instance
Increase the maximum number of instances for the Cloud Run service so more instances are available to handle the load
Switch to an HTTP/1.1 only protocol; HTTP/2 multiplexing causes concurrency issues on Cloud Run
questions.explanation: Two valid fixes for concurrency-driven 429 errors: (1) if the application is not CPU- or memory-bound at 80 concurrent requests, increase the concurrency limit to allow each instance to handle more requests simultaneously; (2) increase the maximum instance count so Cloud Run scales out more instances to handle load peaks. Both approaches increase the total request-handling capacity of the service. Decreasing concurrency to 1 is the opposite of what is needed. HTTP/2 multiplexing is a feature, not a cause of concurrency issues, and Cloud Run fully supports it.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your GKE cluster autoscaler is not scaling down nodes even though CPU utilization is low. Investigation shows that certain pods cannot be rescheduled because they have a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB) with maxUnavailable: 0 and minAvailable equal to the current replica count. What is the effect of this PDB on cluster autoscaler behavior?
options: The cluster autoscaler ignores PodDisruptionBudgets when making scale-down decisions

The PDB prevents the cluster autoscaler from draining pods from the underutilized node because evicting any pod would violate the PDB (minAvailable would not be satisfied); as a result, the node cannot be removed
The PDB only affects rolling updates, not cluster autoscaler scale-down events
The cluster autoscaler will evict pods violating the PDB but add them back on the remaining nodes within 30 seconds
questions.explanation: The cluster autoscaler respects PodDisruptionBudgets when deciding to drain a node for scale-down. If draining a node requires evicting a pod and that eviction would violate the PDB (e.g., minAvailable = replica count means zero pods can be disrupted), the autoscaler cannot evict the pod and therefore cannot drain or remove the node. To allow scale-down, the PDB should be configured to allow at least one pod disruption (e.g., minAvailable = replicas - 1 or maxUnavailable: 1). PDBs are fully respected by the cluster autoscaler in its scale-down calculations.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your application stores user-uploaded images in Cloud Storage. Thumbnails are generated on-demand by a Cloud Run service and served to users. Thumbnail generation takes 800ms. For popular images, the same thumbnail is regenerated on every request. What is the BEST optimization?
options: Increase Cloud Run minimum instances to ensure the thumbnail service is always warm
Generate thumbnails at upload time and store them in Cloud Storage; serve thumbnails directly from Cloud Storage (optionally via Cloud CDN) instead of regenerating on each request
Cache the raw image in Cloud Memorystore so the Cloud Run service reads from Redis instead of Cloud Storage
Use a larger Cloud Run CPU allocation so thumbnail generation takes 200ms instead of 800ms
questions.explanation: Regenerating the same thumbnail on every request wastes compute and adds latency. The correct optimization is to generate thumbnails once (either at upload time via a Cloud Storage trigger or lazily on first request) and store them in Cloud Storage. Subsequent requests serve the pre-generated thumbnail file directly, eliminating the 800ms generation cost. Cloud CDN can further cache frequently accessed thumbnails at the edge. Keeping the Cloud Run service warm reduces cold start latency but not the per-request generation cost. Caching the raw image in Redis speeds up the read of the original but does not eliminate the generation step. CPU upgrades reduce generation time but do not eliminate redundant work.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your team wants to load test a new GKE service before launch. The service must handle 10,000 requests per second with p99 latency under 200ms. You want to run the load test from within GCP to avoid egress charges and network variability from on-premises. Which approach is correct?
options: Use Cloud Scheduler to trigger 10,000 Cloud Run invocations per second as the load generator
Deploy a distributed load testing tool (such as k6, Locust, or JMeter) on GKE in the same VPC as the service under test; scale the load generator pods to generate 10,000 RPS while measuring latency
Enable Cloud CDN and use the CDN's traffic replay feature to simulate 10,000 RPS
Use Cloud Monitoring synthetic monitors at 1-second intervals to generate the required load
questions.explanation: Distributed load testing tools like k6 or Locust, deployed as GKE workloads, can generate high request volumes from within the same VPC as the target service, avoiding egress costs and internet variability. Scaling load generator pods provides horizontal scalability to reach 10,000 RPS. Cloud Scheduler is designed for low-frequency cron jobs and cannot trigger 10,000 parallel HTTP requests per second. Cloud CDN does not have a traffic replay feature. Cloud Monitoring synthetic monitors run at intervals of 1-15 minutes, not 10,000 RPS.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your application running on GKE experiences intermittent latency spikes correlated with garbage collection pauses in the JVM. Cloud Profiler shows GC taking 500ms every 30 seconds. Which TWO JVM tuning strategies can reduce GC pause impact for a latency-sensitive service?
options: Switch from the default G1GC collector to ZGC or Shenandoah, which are low-pause GC algorithms designed to limit stop-the-world pauses to sub-millisecond durations
Increase the JVM heap size (-Xmx) so GC runs less frequently; the pauses will be the same duration but less frequent
Reduce the heap size to force more frequent but shorter GC cycles, improving p99 latency consistency
Set Pod resource limits to request more CPU on GKE; additional CPU allows GC to complete faster
questions.explanation: Two effective approaches: (1) Low-latency GC collectors (ZGC, Shenandoah) are explicitly designed to minimize stop-the-world pauses by doing most GC work concurrently with application threads, reducing pauses from hundreds of milliseconds to single-digit milliseconds. (2) GC is a CPU-intensive operation; if the pod is CPU-throttled (due to low CPU limits), GC threads cannot complete quickly, extending pauses. Ensuring adequate CPU limits allows GC to run at full speed. Increasing heap size reduces GC frequency but does not reduce pause duration per cycle; for a service with 500ms pauses, less frequent 500ms pauses may still be unacceptable. Reducing heap size increases GC frequency, potentially worsening overall throughput.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your application uses Cloud Memorystore for Redis. During peak load, the Redis instance's CPU reaches 80% and latency increases. The workload consists of many small GET and SET operations. Which scaling option for Memorystore for Redis is most appropriate?
options: Enable Redis Cluster mode with sharding; distribute the keyspace across multiple shards to spread CPU and memory load
Increase the Redis instance tier from Basic to Standard; Standard provides higher CPU allocation
Add a read replica to the Redis instance; all GET operations will be served from the replica
Enable the Redis AUTH token; authenticated connections have lower overhead and reduce CPU usage
questions.explanation: Cloud Memorystore for Redis Cluster (sharded mode) distributes the keyspace and command processing across multiple primary shards, multiplying the available CPU and memory capacity linearly with shard count. For CPU-bound workloads with many small operations, horizontal sharding is the correct scaling approach. Memorystore's Basic vs Standard tier difference is high availability (Standard has a replica for failover), not CPU scaling. Read replicas help with read-heavy workloads but a mixed GET/SET workload still sends all SETs to the primary, which is the CPU bottleneck. AUTH has negligible CPU overhead and does not address scaling.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: You are designing a GKE deployment for a batch processing service that only runs during business hours (8 AM - 6 PM on weekdays) and should have zero running pods outside those hours to minimize cost. Which autoscaling approach achieves scheduled scaling with zero pods outside business hours?
options: Set HPA minReplicas to 0 and maxReplicas to 20; Kubernetes will automatically scale to zero when there is no CPU load
Use Cloud Scheduler to trigger a Cloud Run job that calls the Kubernetes API to patch the Deployment's replica count to 0 at 6 PM and to the desired count at 8 AM
Configure Kubernetes Cron Jobs to deploy and delete the Deployment on a schedule
Use KEDA with a cron scaler trigger configured to scale to 0 outside business hours and to the desired replica count during business hours, handled natively by KEDA without external tooling
questions.explanation: KEDA natively supports a cron scaler that scales a Deployment to a specified replica count on a cron schedule, including scaling to 0 outside the defined windows. This is the cleanest solution: no external tooling, the scaling logic lives in a Kubernetes ScaledObject manifest, and KEDA handles the transitions. HPA cannot scale to 0 replicas (minReplicas must be at least 1 unless using KEDA's HPA integration). A Cloud Scheduler + Cloud Run solution works but requires external orchestration and service account management. CronJobs create Job resources, not scale existing Deployments.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your team's Cloud Run service is handling user authentication requests. Response time is critical (target p99 under 50ms). The service validates JWT tokens by fetching the public key from an external JWKS endpoint on every request. The JWKS endpoint has ~20ms latency. How do you eliminate this per-request latency?
options: Increase the Cloud Run instance CPU to make the JWKS fetch faster

Cache the JWKS public keys in the service's in-memory cache with a refresh interval matching the key rotation schedule (e.g., hourly); validate tokens using the cached keys on every request without a network call
Use Cloud Memorystore to store the JWKS response so all Cloud Run instances share a single cached copy
Disable JWT validation in production and rely on network-level mTLS for authentication
questions.explanation: JWKS public keys change rarely (typically on key rotation schedules of hours to days). Caching them in the service's in-memory store with a periodic refresh eliminates the 20ms per-request network call to the JWKS endpoint. In-process caching is faster than Redis (sub-microsecond vs sub-millisecond) and avoids an additional network hop. All JWT validation happens locally using the cached keys. Cloud Memorystore is a valid alternative for sharing keys across a large fleet of instances, but for a single service, in-process caching is simpler and faster. Disabling JWT validation is a security vulnerability. CPU allocation does not reduce network latency.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your organization runs a multi-region GKE application served by a global Application Load Balancer. Users in Europe report consistently higher latency (300ms) compared to US users (50ms). Both regions have GKE backends configured. Cloud Monitoring shows that European users are being routed to the US backend despite the European backend having capacity. What is the MOST likely cause?
options: The European GKE cluster has insufficient CPU; it is failing health checks and the load balancer is routing around it

The load balancer backend service is not configured with a NEG (Network Endpoint Group) for the European cluster, so the load balancer cannot see the European backends and routes all traffic to the US
Cloud CDN is not enabled; enabling it would allow European users to be served from a European edge PoP
The European backend has a higher backend latency than the US backend, so the load balancer's least-latency routing algorithm prefers the US backend
questions.explanation: Global Application Load Balancers use Network Endpoint Groups (NEGs) to route traffic to GKE backends. If the European GKE cluster's pods are not registered in a NEG attached to the backend service, the load balancer has no healthy European endpoints and falls back to the US backend for all traffic. This is a common misconfiguration when adding a second region to an existing load balancer setup. A failing health check (option A) would also cause fallback but the question states the European backend has capacity, suggesting the pods are healthy but not registered. Cloud CDN caches static content but does not resolve backend routing misconfiguration. The load balancer routes to the nearest healthy backend, so if the European NEG is properly registered, it would use it.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Your GKE service is experiencing sudden traffic spikes that cause CPU utilization to exceed 90%. The HPA is configured with a target CPU utilization of 70%. Why might the HPA fail to scale pods fast enough during an abrupt spike?
options: HPA only scales based on memory, not CPU utilization
HPA has a default scale-up stabilization window of 3 minutes and a sync period of 15 seconds, so rapid spikes may cause latency before new pods are ready
HPA requires manual approval before adding pods to avoid cost overruns
HPA cannot scale GKE deployments that use more than one container per pod
questions.explanation: HPA evaluates metrics every 15 seconds (sync period) and by default uses a 3-minute stabilization window before scaling up aggressively. Combined with pod startup and readiness probe delays, sudden traffic spikes can saturate existing pods before new ones are ready. Tuning scaleUp behavior (shorter stabilization window, higher scaling policies) and using VPA for right-sizing reduces this gap. HPA absolutely supports CPU metrics and multi-container pods.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: You want to configure Cloud Run to prevent cold starts from affecting your SLO. Your service processes user-facing requests and must respond within 200ms at the 99th percentile. Which Cloud Run setting MOST directly reduces cold-start impact?
options: Increase the maximum number of instances to 1000
Set min-instances to a value that keeps enough instances warm to handle expected baseline traffic without cold starts
Enable HTTP/2 end-to-end on the Cloud Run service
Reduce the container memory allocation so instances start faster
questions.explanation: Cloud Run min-instances keeps a configured number of instances running and warm, meaning they have completed initialization and are ready to serve requests immediately. This directly eliminates cold starts for traffic up to the capacity of the warm instances. Setting max-instances high only helps at peak scale but does not prevent cold starts. HTTP/2 improves multiplexing but does not affect startup latency. Reducing memory allocation may speed up memory allocation but container init time is dominated by application startup code, not memory size.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your GKE Deployment currently has VPA configured in "Off" mode. Engineers want VPA to automatically update running pod resource requests without manual rollouts. What VPA update mode should they set, and what is the operational implication?
options: Set VPA to "Initial" mode; VPA will update CPU and memory requests at runtime without restarting pods
Set VPA to "Auto" mode; VPA will evict and reschedule pods to apply updated resource requests, causing brief pod restarts
Set VPA to "Recreate" mode; VPA will drain the entire node before applying updates
Set VPA to "Live" mode; VPA will hot-patch resource limits in place without any disruption
questions.explanation: VPA "Auto" mode applies its recommendations by evicting pods so they are recreated with the updated resource requests. This causes brief pod restarts, so proper PodDisruptionBudgets are essential to maintain availability. "Initial" mode only applies recommendations when new pods are created (not to running pods). "Recreate" is not a VPA mode name. There is no "Live" hot-patch mode for VPA.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team observes that the error rate for a GKE microservice spiked to 8% for 4 minutes, then recovered. The SLO is 99.5% availability over a 30-day window. The error budget is 65% consumed before this incident. Which statement BEST describes the impact of this event on the error budget?
options: The 4-minute spike consumed no error budget because the service recovered automatically
The spike consumed error budget proportional to the 8% error rate across those 4 minutes, which must be subtracted from the 30-day error budget calculation
The spike reset the error budget window back to zero since an SLO violation occurred
Error budget is only consumed when the service is completely unavailable, not during partial error spikes
questions.explanation: Error budget is consumed continuously based on the ratio of bad requests to total requests. A 4-minute period at 8% error rate contributes bad events during those minutes to the 30-day rolling calculation. The exact budget consumed depends on traffic volume during the spike. Error budget is not binary; partial degradation counts. The window does not reset; it rolls. Any request failure that violates the SLI definition consumes budget, whether partial or complete outage.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A Cloud Run service is experiencing elevated latency. You observe that the p50 latency is 80ms, p95 is 350ms, and p99 is 2400ms. The service calls a downstream Cloud SQL instance. Which of the four golden signals does this pattern MOST suggest as the primary area to investigate?
options: Traffic: the service is receiving more requests than expected
Saturation: the downstream Cloud SQL connection pool or CPU is becoming saturated under load, causing tail latency inflation
Errors: the high p99 indicates requests are failing silently
Availability: the service is completely unreachable for some users
questions.explanation: The wide gap between p50 (80ms) and p99 (2400ms) is a classic saturation signature: the majority of requests complete quickly but a tail of requests waits for a resource (connection pool slots, CPU time, lock contention) to free up. This points to saturation in the downstream Cloud SQL connection pool or instance CPU. Traffic volume alone would shift all percentiles up proportionally. High p99 with normal error rate is not an errors signal. Availability issues manifest as connection refusals or timeouts across all percentiles.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: During an incident review, your team discovers that a deployment caused elevated 5xx errors for 12 minutes before anyone was paged. The alerting policy was set to fire when error rate exceeded 1% for 10 consecutive minutes. What change would BEST reduce detection time while avoiding false positives?
options: Lower the error threshold to 0.1% with the same 10-minute duration window
Reduce the duration to 2 minutes and implement a burn-rate-based alert that fires when the error budget is being consumed at 14x the normal rate
Add a second alert that pages immediately on any single 5xx response
Switch to a log-based alert that counts 5xx entries and pages when count exceeds 50 per minute
questions.explanation: Burn-rate alerting (as defined in the SRE workbook) fires when the error budget is being consumed at a multiple of the sustainable rate. A 14x burn rate alert with a short window (2 minutes) detects fast-moving outages quickly while the high multiplier prevents false positives from normal traffic variance. Lowering the threshold to 0.1% with a 10-minute window misses fast incidents. Paging on any single 5xx creates alert fatigue. Log-based count thresholds require manual tuning for traffic volume changes.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your SRE team wants to reduce toil in the on-call rotation. Engineers are spending 3 hours per week manually restarting pods that enter a CrashLoopBackOff state due to a known transient dependency timeout. Which approach BEST reduces this toil?
options: Schedule a weekly maintenance window to restart all pods proactively
Implement a Kubernetes liveness probe tuned to detect the failure pattern, so the kubelet automatically restarts the affected containers without human intervention
Create a runbook entry documenting the restart steps so on-call engineers can execute it faster
Migrate all pods to Cloud Run so GCP manages restarts automatically
questions.explanation: Toil is repetitive, automatable, manual work that grows with scale. Configuring a Kubernetes liveness probe that detects the stuck state allows the kubelet to automatically restart the container, eliminating the manual intervention entirely. Proactive restarts treat the symptom but are still manual toil. Runbooks reduce execution time but do not eliminate toil. Migrating to Cloud Run may help but is a much larger change than fixing the probe, and Cloud Run does not directly solve upstream dependency timeouts.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team wants to implement self-healing for a situation where a Cloud Spanner region becomes unavailable and requests to your GKE service start failing. What is the MOST automated and resilient approach?
options: Configure a Cloud Monitoring alert that pages the on-call engineer to manually switch connection strings
Use Cloud Spanner multi-region configurations with automatic failover; configure your application to use the multi-region endpoint so the client library automatically routes to available replicas
Write a Cloud Functions function triggered by a log-based alert that updates a Secret Manager secret with the fallback connection string
Deploy a second application stack in a different region and use manual DNS failover when the primary region fails
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner multi-region instance configurations replicate data across multiple regions and the client library's multi-region endpoint automatically routes read-write requests to the closest available leader and retries on regional failures. No application-level failover code or human intervention is needed. Manual on-call intervention, secret patching via Cloud Functions, and manual DNS failover all introduce MTTR delays and operational toil.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your organization wants to conduct a chaos engineering experiment on a GKE service. The service has a dependency on a Cloud Pub/Sub subscription. You want to simulate message processing delays without affecting production traffic. Which approach is MOST appropriate for a safe chaos experiment?
options: Inject random sleep() calls directly into the production consumer code and deploy to production

Create a separate staging environment that mirrors production topology; use a chaos testing tool or custom test harness to artificially delay Pub/Sub message acknowledgements in staging, then measure the impact on downstream service health
Stop the Pub/Sub subscription in production during low-traffic hours and observe behavior
Delete and recreate the Pub/Sub topic to simulate message loss in production
questions.explanation: Chaos engineering experiments must be conducted with a hypothesis, in a controlled environment (preferably staging that mirrors production), and with a defined blast radius. Running experiments in a staging environment allows you to observe failure modes without user impact. Injecting delays in production code, stopping production subscriptions, or deleting production topics all risk real user harm and violate the safe chaos principle of minimal blast radius and reversibility.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your company requires a Recovery Time Objective (RTO) of 15 minutes and a Recovery Point Objective (RPO) of 5 minutes for a critical Cloud SQL database. Which backup and failover configuration BEST meets these requirements?
options: Enable daily automated backups with a retention of 7 days; manually restore from the latest backup during a failure event
Enable Cloud SQL high availability (HA) with a standby instance in the same zone, and enable automated backups every 4 hours
Enable Cloud SQL HA with a standby instance in a different zone, enable automated backups, and enable point-in-time recovery (PITR) with transaction log retention of at least 5 minutes
Use Cloud SQL read replicas in two regions and promote the replica manually when the primary fails
questions.explanation: Cloud SQL HA (zonal failover to a standby in a different zone) provides automatic failover in under 60 seconds, meeting the 15-minute RTO. PITR with transaction logs retained every 5 minutes allows recovery to within 5 minutes of a failure, meeting the RPO. Daily backups and manual restore cannot meet 15-minute RTO. Same-zone HA does not protect against zonal failures. Cross-region read replica promotion is manual and slower than HA failover.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your service calls three downstream microservices. One of them (Service C) experiences periodic slowdowns. Without a circuit breaker, how does Service C slowness affect your service, and what does a circuit breaker prevent?
options: Service C slowness only affects Service C itself; your service is unaffected because HTTP calls are stateless

Service C slowness causes your service threads or goroutines to block waiting for responses, exhausting connection pools and causing cascading latency; a circuit breaker opens after a threshold of failures, failing fast and allowing graceful degradation
A circuit breaker automatically fixes Service C by restarting its pods when it detects slowness
Circuit breakers only apply to database connections, not HTTP service-to-service calls
questions.explanation: When Service C is slow, your service blocks on outbound HTTP calls, consuming threads or goroutines. If enough concurrent requests wait on Service C, your service exhausts its connection pool or request handling capacity, making it slow or unavailable to all callers regardless of whether they need Service C. A circuit breaker tracks failure or timeout rates; when they exceed a threshold, it opens and immediately returns an error (fail fast) without attempting the call. This prevents thread exhaustion and allows graceful degradation such as returning cached data or a fallback response.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: After a significant incident, your team holds a postmortem. The primary contributing factor was a deployment that bypassed the staging environment. Which action item MOST effectively prevents recurrence at the systemic level?
options: Write a postmortem document and share it with the team for awareness
Send an email reminder to all engineers to always deploy to staging first
Enforce staging deployment as a required pipeline stage in Cloud Deploy, blocking production promotion until staging deployment succeeds and smoke tests pass
Add a checklist item to the deployment runbook to remind engineers to deploy to staging
questions.explanation: Effective postmortem action items are systemic fixes, not behavioral reminders. Enforcing staging as a required pipeline stage in Cloud Deploy means the system makes it impossible to promote to production without first deploying and validating in staging. This eliminates the human error vector. Awareness emails, postmortem documents, and runbook checklists are all process controls that rely on human compliance and will eventually be bypassed again.

questions.question: Your team uses a 28-day rolling error budget. Six days ago, an incident consumed 60% of the remaining budget. Your SLO review is monthly. What are TWO appropriate responses to this budget consumption? (Choose TWO)
options: Freeze all new feature releases and focus exclusively on reliability improvements until the budget recovers
Conduct a postmortem on the incident and implement action items to prevent recurrence before the next SLO review
Adjust the SLO target downward to retroactively give back the consumed budget
Increase error budget monitoring alert thresholds so the team is notified earlier when budget burn accelerates
questions.explanation: When significant error budget is consumed, the SRE model calls for two complementary responses: (1) freezing new feature work to protect remaining budget and stabilize the service, and (2) conducting a postmortem with concrete action items to prevent recurrence. Retroactively lowering the SLO to recover budget is gaming the system and invalidates the SLO contract with users. Raising alert thresholds makes monitoring less sensitive, which is the opposite of what is needed after an incident.

questions.question: Your team wants to forecast when the current Cloud Spanner instance capacity will be saturated to proactively plan quota increases. Which approach provides the MOST actionable forecast?
options: Wait until CPU utilization exceeds 80% on a single day, then immediately request quota

Export Cloud Monitoring metrics for Spanner CPU and query utilization to BigQuery; use regression analysis or trend forecasting on the historical data to project when utilization will reach the target threshold
Use Cloud Billing reports to predict when Spanner costs will exceed budget as a proxy for capacity
Set a Cloud Monitoring alert at 75% CPU and treat each alert as a capacity planning trigger
questions.explanation: Proactive capacity planning requires trend analysis over historical utilization data. Exporting Cloud Monitoring metrics to BigQuery allows the team to apply regression or time-series forecasting to predict when a metric will reach a critical threshold, enabling advance quota requests. Reactive single-day thresholds are too late for quota approvals. Billing reports reflect cost, not resource saturation patterns. Alert-based triggers are reactive, not predictive.

questions.question: Your load testing reveals that your GKE service handles 5,000 RPS before latency at the p99 exceeds your SLO threshold. Current peak production traffic is 3,200 RPS. Which action BEST ensures the service stays within SLO as traffic grows while minimizing over-provisioning?
options: Immediately scale the deployment to handle 10,000 RPS to provide ample headroom
Set HPA to scale out when CPU utilization exceeds 60%, configure a capacity alert when RPS reaches 4,000 (80% of tested capacity), and schedule a re-load-test when headroom shrinks below 20%
Disable autoscaling and manually add pods monthly based on traffic growth reports
Set the HPA maximum replicas to exactly match the current traffic of 3,200 RPS worth of pods
questions.explanation: Effective capacity management combines proactive alerting (notify before hitting tested limits), autoscaling (handle organic growth automatically), and scheduled re-validation (re-load-test to ensure new code does not regress capacity). Setting an alert at 80% of tested capacity gives the team time to investigate before the SLO is at risk. Immediately scaling to 10,000 RPS wastes resources. Manual monthly scaling is too slow for traffic spikes. Capping HPA replicas at current traffic prevents scaling to handle growth.
